mnemonics.title: Minimization Steps
mnemonics.content: START: Separate Accept from Reject; SPLIT: Check transitions to different groups; STOP: When groups stop changing.

mnemonics.title: Distinguishable vs Equivalent
mnemonics.content: DIstinguishable = Different behavior for Some string; Equivalent = Same behavior for All strings. D = Different Some, E = Equal All.

mnemonics.title: Table Filling
mnemonics.content: Mark Accept≠Reject pairs first, then check transitions - if targets marked, mark sources. Keep marking until no new marks.

mnemonics.title: Myhill-Nerode
mnemonics.content: Myhill-Nerode: Minimum States = Number of Distinct Equivalence Classes. More classes = more memory needed.

acronyms.usage: The type of automaton that is minimized using partition refinement or table-filling methods.
Converted to DFA (via subset construction) before minimization is applied.
General term for both DFA and NFA; minimization applies specifically to deterministic versions.

visualTips.visualization: Think of dividing states into boxes. Initially: Box 1 = all accepting states, Box 2 = all non-accepting. Then repeatedly check if states in a box behave differently (go to different boxes on same input). If yes, split the box. Stop when no box can be split.

Imagine two detectives following identical paths. If they always reach the same conclusion (accept/reject) regardless of what path they take, they're equivalent. If one can catch the other in a lie (different conclusion), they're distinguishable.

Create an n×n grid with states as rows/columns. Initially mark cells where one state accepts and other doesn't. Then for each unmarked pair, check if their transitions on any input go to marked pairs. Keep marking until stable.
Each state in minimal DFA represents a unique 'memory' needed to recognize the language. Fewer states = less memory needed. Two states that seem different but do same thing can be merged.

analogies.analogy: Two employees are distinguishable if one can be caught making a mistake while the other doesn't - they handle at least one situation differently. They're equivalent if they always handle all situations identically.
Like organizing a team where people who always do the same tasks are merged into one role. If two team members produce identical results in all scenarios, they're redundant.
A dead state is like a conversation ending in 'no' - once you reach it, no matter what else is said, the answer remains 'no'.
Each equivalence class is like a unique 'context' or 'memory state' needed. If the language needs to remember 5 different things, minimum 5 states required.
